result:
:::writing
There is a particular kind of quiet that only exists just before something begins.

It’s not the quiet of an empty room or the hush of snowfall, but a charged stillness, like the pause between lightning and thunder, or the held breath before a diver slips beneath the surface. It feels as if the world itself is listening, waiting for its cue. People rarely notice it, because we are so good at filling silence—with noise, with thought, with distraction—but it is there, flickering at the edges of experience.

I first became aware of it on an ordinary Tuesday morning, the kind that usually dissolves without leaving a trace. I was standing at a bus stop, half-awake, watching condensation slide down the side of a paper coffee cup. Cars moved past in a steady rhythm. Someone nearby was scrolling through their phone, thumb moving with practiced indifference. The sky was undecided, hovering between gray and blue.

And then, without warning, everything seemed to pause.

Not physically—cars still moved, people still shifted their weight, the world did not freeze—but something underneath it all went still. It was as if a layer of sound had been peeled away, revealing a deeper silence beneath. Even my own thoughts seemed to hesitate, like they had lost their footing.

It lasted maybe two seconds.

Then a bus rounded the corner, brakes hissing, doors folding open, and the moment snapped shut like it had never been there.

I didn’t think much of it at the time. A trick of attention, I figured. A momentary lapse in the usual stream of consciousness. But over the following weeks, I began to notice it again. Not often, and never predictably, but enough to feel like a pattern rather than coincidence.

It would happen in small, in-between moments. Waiting for a crosswalk signal. Sitting in a café just before the barista called a name. Standing in line at a grocery store while the person ahead of me fumbled for their wallet. Always brief. Always subtle. Always gone before I could examine it too closely.

At first, I tried to ignore it. There’s a natural instinct to dismiss anything that doesn’t fit neatly into explanation. But curiosity has a way of eroding resistance. The more I noticed these moments, the more I wanted to understand them.

So I started paying attention.

Not in an obsessive way, but gently, like you might watch for fireflies on a summer evening. I stopped reaching for my phone in idle moments. I let silence stretch a little longer before filling it. I listened—to the hum of fluorescent lights, the distant murmur of conversation, the almost imperceptible rhythm of my own breathing.

And slowly, the moments began to change.

They didn’t become longer, exactly, but they became clearer. More defined. Instead of slipping past unnoticed, they stood out, like punctuation marks in the flow of time. A comma here. A semicolon there. Occasionally, something closer to a period.

One afternoon, I experienced it more strongly than ever before.

I was walking through a park, cutting across a patch of grass to save time. It was early spring, the kind of day where winter hasn’t quite released its grip but the air carries a hint of something softer. People were scattered around—some sitting on benches, others walking dogs, a few lying on the grass as if testing whether the ground had finally forgiven them for winter.

I remember noticing a child running, arms outstretched, chasing nothing in particular. I remember the sound of wind moving through bare branches. I remember thinking about something trivial—what to make for dinner, maybe, or an email I needed to send.

And then it happened.

But this time, instead of passing quickly, it deepened.

The quiet didn’t just arrive—it expanded. It filled the space around me, settled into it. The usual background noise of the world softened, as if someone had turned down a dial I didn’t know existed. Even my thoughts seemed to slow, then stop, like a machine gently powering down.

For a moment—longer than before, though I couldn’t say how long—I felt completely suspended.

Not frozen. Not disconnected. Just… present in a way that felt unfamiliar.

The child was still running. The wind was still moving. The world hadn’t stopped. But my relationship to it had shifted, like I had stepped half an inch outside the usual flow of experience.

There was no urgency in that space. No sense of before or after. Just a simple, undeniable awareness.

Then, just as gently as it had arrived, it receded.

Sound returned to its normal volume. Thoughts resumed their usual chatter. The moment closed, leaving behind a faint impression, like the echo of a dream you can’t quite hold onto.

I stood there for a few seconds, unsure of what had just happened.

It would be easy to frame it as something mystical, something rare or special. But it didn’t feel that way. If anything, it felt ordinary—so ordinary that it bordered on invisible. Like I had briefly noticed something that is always there, just usually overlooked.

That idea stayed with me.

What if these moments weren’t interruptions, but glimpses? Not anomalies, but reminders? What if the “charged quiet” I had been noticing wasn’t a break in reality, but a layer of it?

It’s strange to consider how much of life is spent in anticipation or recollection. We are constantly leaning forward into what comes next or backward into what has already happened. Even when we think we are present, our attention is often fragmented—split between thought, memory, expectation, and distraction.

But in those quiet moments, all of that seems to fall away.

There is no next. No before. Just the immediate, unfiltered experience of being.

I began to wonder if this was something everyone experienced, or if it was something people learned to notice over time. Maybe it happens to children more often, before they develop the habit of constant mental narration. Maybe it fades as we grow older, replaced by the steady hum of responsibility and routine.

Or maybe it never fades at all.

Maybe it’s always there, threading through our days, appearing in the spaces we usually overlook. The pause before someone speaks. The split second before a song begins. The moment just after waking, when the world hasn’t fully assembled itself yet.

If that’s the case, then it’s not something we need to find. It’s something we need to notice.

That idea is both comforting and unsettling.

Comforting, because it suggests that there is a kind of stillness available to us at any moment, no matter how chaotic things may seem. Unsettling, because it implies that we are constantly brushing past it without realizing, missing something fundamental in the process.

I don’t think noticing it changes your life in any dramatic, cinematic way. There’s no sudden transformation, no instant clarity about everything that matters. You still have responsibilities, worries, plans. You still get caught up in thought, still lose yourself in distraction.

But it does something subtle.

It creates a small gap.

A bit of space between you and the constant stream of thinking. A reminder that not every moment needs to be filled, explained, or optimized. That there is value in simply experiencing something without immediately turning it into something else.

In a world that encourages constant engagement, that kind of space can feel almost radical.

We are taught, in countless ways, to maximize our time. To be productive, efficient, informed. Even our leisure is often structured, curated, measured. There’s always something to do, something to watch, something to check.

And in the midst of all that, silence can feel uncomfortable.

Not because there is anything inherently wrong with it, but because we are not used to it. We have trained ourselves to associate silence with absence, with lack, rather than recognizing it as something with its own texture and presence.

But those moments—the charged quiet, the subtle pauses—suggest otherwise.

They hint at a different way of relating to time. Not as something to be filled or managed, but as something to be experienced more directly.

I don’t think this means abandoning structure or responsibility. It’s not about rejecting the practical realities of life. It’s about noticing that beneath all of it, there is another layer—a quieter one—that is always available.

And sometimes, if you happen to catch it at the right moment, it feels like the world is holding its breath.

Not in tension, but in anticipation.

As if something is about to begin.

Maybe that’s what those moments are.

Not pauses in the story, but thresholds.

Tiny, nearly invisible doorways between one moment and the next. Points where the future hasn’t fully formed yet, where possibility still feels open and undefined.

We usually pass through them without noticing, carried forward by habit and momentum. But every now and then, if we’re paying attention, we catch a glimpse of the transition itself.

And in that glimpse, there is something quietly extraordinary.

Not because it is rare, but because it is always there.

Waiting.

For just a second, the world softens its edges, and you find yourself standing in that in-between space, aware of something you can’t quite name.

Then it passes.

The bus arrives. The light changes. Someone calls your name.

And the story continues.
:::writing

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
